facts.text: Saving Private Ryan (1998) opens with a 27-minute depiction of the Omaha Beach assault that is widely considered the most realistic combat sequence ever filmed.
Steven Spielberg deliberately left the lens uncoated on his cameras during the D-Day sequence in Saving Private Ryan to replicate the desaturated look of 1940s newsreel footage.
The cast of Saving Private Ryan—minus Matt Damon—underwent a grueling 10-day boot camp run by Marine veteran Dale Dye before filming began.
Matt Damon was intentionally excluded from the boot camp so the other actors would resent his character, creating authentic tension on screen.
Saving Private Ryan was inspired by the real story of the Niland brothers, four siblings who served in World War II, two of whom were believed killed in action.
Saving Private Ryan earned five Academy Awards, including Best Director for Steven Spielberg, but lost Best Picture to Shakespeare in Love.
After the release of Saving Private Ryan, the Department of Veterans Affairs set up a special hotline because so many WWII veterans were traumatized by the film's realism.
Schindler's List (1993) was shot almost entirely in black and white to evoke the documentary footage of the Holocaust era.
Steven Spielberg refused to accept a salary for directing Schindler's List, calling it 'blood money,' and donated all his profits to the Shoah Foundation.
The girl in the red coat in Schindler's List is one of the film's few color elements, symbolizing the innocence lost during the Holocaust.
Schindler's List won seven Academy Awards, including Best Picture and Best Director.
Liam Neeson lost 30 pounds to portray the gaunt Oskar Schindler in the later scenes of the film.
Real Schindler survivors and their families appear in the final scene of Schindler's List, placing stones on Oskar Schindler's grave in Jerusalem.
The Pianist (2002) is based on the autobiography of Władysław Szpilman, a Polish-Jewish pianist who survived the destruction of the Warsaw Ghetto.
Adrien Brody gave up his apartment, sold his car, and isolated himself for months to prepare for his role in The Pianist.
Adrien Brody became the youngest actor to win the Best Actor Oscar at age 29 for The Pianist.
Director Roman Polanski drew on his own childhood experiences surviving the Kraków Ghetto while making The Pianist.
Dunkirk (2017) used minimal dialogue and relied on Hans Zimmer's ticking-clock score to create relentless tension throughout the film.
Christopher Nolan structured Dunkirk using three interlocking timelines: one week on the beach, one day at sea, and one hour in the air.
Nolan used real Spitfires and a decommissioned French destroyer for the filming of Dunkirk rather than relying on CGI.
Over 60 real small boats, many of them original 'little ships' from the 1940 evacuation, were used in the filming of Dunkirk.
Dunkirk used IMAX cameras for roughly 75% of the film, making it one of the most extensive uses of the large-format cameras in a narrative feature.
Band of Brothers (2001) is a 10-part HBO miniseries based on historian Stephen Ambrose's book about Easy Company, 506th Parachute Infantry Regiment.
Band of Brothers cost an estimated $125 million to produce, making it the most expensive television production of its time.
The actors in Band of Brothers attended a similar boot camp to the one used for Saving Private Ryan, overseen by the same military advisor, Dale Dye.
Several real members of Easy Company appear in interview segments at the beginning of each Band of Brothers episode, though their identities are not revealed until the finale.
Hacksaw Ridge (2016) tells the true story of Desmond Doss, the first conscientious objector to receive the Medal of Honor for actions during the Battle of Okinawa.
Desmond Doss single-handedly rescued an estimated 75 wounded soldiers from the battlefield at Hacksaw Ridge without carrying a weapon.
Andrew Garfield lost 40 pounds and trained extensively to portray Desmond Doss in Hacksaw Ridge.
Mel Gibson directed Hacksaw Ridge, marking his return to directing after a 10-year hiatus following Apocalypse (2006).
Hacksaw Ridge was filmed in New South Wales, Australia, standing in for the cliffs of Okinawa, Japan.
Apocalypse Now (1979) began as a screenplay by John Milius, loosely adapted from Joseph Conrad's novella Heart of Darkness.
The production of Apocalypse Now was so troubled that Francis Ford Coppola famously said, 'We had access to too much money, too much equipment, and little by little, we went insane.'
Martin Sheen suffered a heart attack during the filming of Apocalypse Now and had to crawl half a mile to get help.
Marlon Brando arrived on the set of Apocalypse Now significantly overweight and reportedly had not read Conrad's Heart of Darkness, forcing Coppola to improvise much of Colonel Kurtz's dialogue.
A typhoon destroyed many of the sets for Apocalypse Now in the Philippines, causing months of delays during production.
Apocalypse Now won the Palme d'Or at the Cannes Film Festival in 1979, shared with The Tin Drum.
The famous helicopter attack sequence in Apocalypse Now uses Richard Wagner's 'Ride of the Valkyries,' which has since become one of the most iconic music-to-film pairings in cinema.
The Philippines government occasionally recalled the helicopters lent to the Apocalypse Now production to fight actual insurgents.
Apocalypse Now Redux (2001) restored 49 minutes of footage cut from the original theatrical release.
Platoon (1986) was based on Oliver Stone's own experiences as an infantryman in the Vietnam War.
Oliver Stone served with the 25th Infantry Division near the Cambodian border in 1967-68, and many events in Platoon reflect his real combat experiences.
Platoon won four Academy Awards, including Best Picture and Best Director for Oliver Stone.
Charlie Sheen, Willem Dafoe, and the rest of the Platoon cast trained for two weeks in the Philippine jungle, sleeping in foxholes and eating military rations.
The cast of Platoon was deliberately divided into two squads that were kept separate to build the rivalry depicted in the film.
Full Metal Jacket (1987) was Stanley Kubrick's Vietnam War film, adapted from Gustav Hasford's novel The Short-Timers.
Full Metal Jacket was filmed entirely in England; Kubrick used a derelict gasworks in East London and imported 200 palm trees to simulate Vietnam.
R. Lee Ermey, a real former Marine drill instructor, improvised much of his dialogue as Gunnery Sergeant Hartman in Full Metal Jacket.
Stanley Kubrick reportedly made R. Lee Ermey film over 100 takes of individual scenes to get the exact performance he wanted.
The barracks scenes in Full Metal Jacket were filmed at a former Royal Air Force base in Cambridgeshire, England.
The Deer Hunter (1978) was one of the first major Hollywood films to address the psychological trauma of the Vietnam War.
The Russian roulette scenes in The Deer Hunter, while not historically documented as a widespread practice, became one of the most intense and debated sequences in war film history.
The Deer Hunter won five Academy Awards, including Best Picture and Best Director for Michael Cimino.
Robert De Niro insisted on wearing a real Green Beret uniform for The Deer Hunter and trained with actual Special Forces soldiers.
Good Morning, Vietnam (1987) starred Robin Williams as Armed Forces Radio DJ Adrian Cronauer, and much of Williams's on-air dialogue was improvised.
Robin Williams reportedly produced so much improvised material for Good Morning, Vietnam that director Barry Levinson had hours of unused footage.
The real Adrian Cronauer has said that the events in Good Morning, Vietnam were heavily fictionalized; he was never removed from the air for his broadcasts.
1917 (2019) was designed to appear as if it were filmed in a single continuous take, using long takes stitched together with hidden cuts.
Director Sam Mendes was inspired to make 1917 by stories his grandfather told him about serving as a messenger on the Western Front in World War I.
Cinematographer Roger Deakins won the Academy Award for Best Cinematography for 1917, using elaborate choreography to achieve the one-shot effect.
The trenches built for 1917 stretched over a mile across farmland in Wiltshire and Hankley Common in England.
1917 required the actors to rehearse their movements repeatedly so the camera crew could follow them through elaborately staged environments in real time.
All Quiet on the Western Front (1930) was one of the first major anti-war films, based on Erich Maria Remarque's novel about German soldiers in World War I.
All Quiet on the Western Front (1930) won the Academy Award for Best Picture, making it one of the earliest war films to receive the honor.
The Nazis banned All Quiet on the Western Front in Germany and disrupted screenings by releasing stink bombs and white mice in theaters.
A 2022 German-language adaptation of All Quiet on the Western Front won four Academy Awards, including Best International Feature Film.
The 2022 All Quiet on the Western Front was the first German-language film to be nominated for the Best Picture Oscar.
War Horse (2011) was Steven Spielberg's World War I film, adapted from Michael Morpurgo's novel and the subsequent stage play.
Spielberg used real horses extensively in War Horse rather than CGI, employing animatronic horses only for the most dangerous scenes.
The stage version of War Horse used life-size horse puppets operated by three puppeteers each, and the technique influenced Spielberg's approach to the film.
Black Hawk Down (2001) depicts the 1993 Battle of Mogadishu in Somalia and was directed by Ridley Scott.
The real-life Battle of Mogadishu depicted in Black Hawk Down lasted 15 hours and resulted in 18 American soldiers killed and 73 wounded.
Ridley Scott had the cast of Black Hawk Down undergo intense military training with actual Army Rangers and Delta Force operators.
Black Hawk Down was filmed in Morocco, not Somalia, due to the instability in the Horn of Africa region.
Several veterans of the actual Battle of Mogadishu served as technical advisors on the set of Black Hawk Down.
American Sniper (2014) is based on the autobiography of Chris Kyle, the most lethal sniper in U.S. military history with 160 confirmed kills.
Bradley Cooper gained 40 pounds of muscle to portray Chris Kyle in American Sniper and trained with Navy SEALs for the role.
American Sniper became the highest-grossing war film in U.S. box office history, earning over $350 million domestically.
Clint Eastwood directed American Sniper when he was 84 years old, making it one of his most commercially successful films.
A controversial fake baby prop used in one scene of American Sniper became an internet meme and prompted Clint Eastwood to explain it was a last-minute replacement when real infants were unavailable.
The Hurt Locker (2008) follows a U.S. Army Explosive Ordnance Disposal team during the Iraq War.
Kathryn Bigelow became the first woman to win the Academy Award for Best Director for The Hurt Locker.
The Hurt Locker beat Avatar for Best Picture despite earning only about $17 million at the domestic box office compared to Avatar's $2.7 billion worldwide.
The Hurt Locker was filmed in Jordan, just miles from the Iraqi border, to achieve authentic desert locations.
Lone Survivor (2013) depicts Operation Red Wings in Afghanistan, where a four-man Navy SEAL team was ambushed by Taliban fighters.
Marcus Luttrell, the lone survivor of Operation Red Wings, served as a technical advisor on the set and was present for most of the filming.
Mark Wahlberg, who played Marcus Luttrell in Lone Survivor, trained with Luttrell himself to learn proper SEAL tactics and movements.
Zero Dark Thirty (2012) chronicles the decade-long hunt for Osama bin Laden, culminating in the Navy SEAL raid on his compound in Abbottabad, Pakistan.
Jessica Chastain's portrayal of a CIA analyst in Zero Dark Thirty was reportedly based on a real, unnamed female officer who was instrumental in locating Bin Laden.
Zero Dark Thirty sparked Congressional investigations into whether the CIA provided classified information to the filmmakers.
The Bridge on the River Kwai (1957) won seven Academy Awards, including Best Picture and Best Actor for Alec Guinness.
The real bridge over the River Kwai was part of the Burma Railway, built by forced labor during World War II, resulting in the deaths of an estimated 12,000 prisoners of war.
Director David Lean built a real bridge for the climax of The Bridge on the River Kwai, which was then destroyed on camera using actual explosives.
Alec Guinness initially wanted to turn down the role of Colonel Nicholson in The Bridge on the River Kwai because he felt the character was too unsympathetic.
The Great Escape (1963) is based on the true story of a mass escape by Allied prisoners of war from the German POW camp Stalag Luft III in 1944.
Steve McQueen's famous motorcycle chase in The Great Escape did not happen in real life; it was added to give McQueen a more prominent action sequence.
Steve McQueen performed many of his own motorcycle stunts in The Great Escape, though his stunt double Bud Ekins executed the iconic fence jump.
Of the 76 men who escaped in the real Great Escape, 73 were recaptured and 50 were executed on Hitler's direct orders.
Patton (1970) opens with George C. Scott delivering a six-minute monologue in front of a giant American flag, which has become one of the most iconic scenes in cinema history.
George C. Scott won the Academy Award for Best Actor for Patton but refused to accept it, calling the ceremony a 'meat parade.'
Patton was reportedly one of Richard Nixon's favorite films; he screened it multiple times at the White House.
The Longest Day (1962) depicted the D-Day invasion using an ensemble cast that included John Wayne, Henry Fonda, Sean Connery, and Robert Mitchum.
The Longest Day used nearly 23,000 extras, many of them actual soldiers from multiple nations' armed forces.
Tora! Tora! Tora! (1970) depicted the attack on Pearl Harbor from both American and Japanese perspectives, using separate American and Japanese directors.
Akira Kurosawa was originally set to direct the Japanese segments of Tora! Tora! Tora! but was replaced after being deemed difficult to work with by the studio.
Tora! Tora! Tora! used full-scale replica Japanese aircraft built from modified American trainers for the attack sequences.
The Thin Red Line (1998) was director Terrence Malick's return to filmmaking after a 20-year absence, telling the story of the Battle of Guadalcanal.
The Thin Red Line featured an enormous ensemble cast including Sean Penn, Jim Caviezel, Nick Nolte, Adrien Brody, George Clooney, and John Travolta.
Adrien Brody believed he had the lead role in The Thin Red Line but discovered at the premiere that most of his scenes had been cut.
The Thin Red Line was released the same year as Saving Private Ryan, leading to inevitable comparisons between the two WWII films.
Letters from Iwo Jima (2006) was directed by Clint Eastwood and depicted the Battle of Iwo Jima entirely from the Japanese perspective, in Japanese with English subtitles.
Letters from Iwo Jima was a companion film to Flags of Our Fathers (2006), also directed by Eastwood, which told the American side of the Battle of Iwo Jima.
Clint Eastwood directed both Flags of Our Fathers and Letters from Iwo Jima in the same year, an unusual achievement for any filmmaker.
Ken Watanabe received widespread critical acclaim for portraying General Tadamichi Kuribayashi in Letters from Iwo Jima.
Come and See (1985), a Soviet film about the Nazi occupation of Belarus, is considered one of the most harrowing and realistic war films ever made.
Director Elem Klimov used live ammunition fired over actors' heads during the filming of Come and See to capture genuine reactions of terror.
Das Boot (1981) follows the crew of a German U-boat during World War II and is regarded as one of the greatest submarine films ever made.
The interior of the U-boat set for Das Boot was built to actual size, forcing actors and crew to work in extremely confined spaces for months.
Das Boot was originally produced as a six-hour German TV miniseries before being edited down to a theatrical release.
Downfall (2004) depicts the final days of Adolf Hitler in his Berlin bunker, with Bruno Ganz delivering a widely acclaimed performance as Hitler.
Bruno Ganz spent months studying footage and recordings of Hitler to perfect his portrayal in Downfall, including mimicking Hitler's speaking patterns and mannerisms.
A scene from Downfall of Hitler ranting became one of the most popular internet memes, with users adding humorous subtitles to the clip.
Fury (2014) starred Brad Pitt as a World War II tank commander and featured a real Tiger I tank, the only operational one in the world, from the Bovington Tank Museum.
The Tiger 131 used in Fury had been captured from German forces in Tunisia in 1943 and carefully restored over many years.
Shia LaBeouf reportedly pulled out his own tooth, cut his face with a knife, and refused to shower for weeks to stay in character during the filming of Fury.
Pearl Harbor (2001) directed by Michael Bay cost over $140 million to make, making it one of the most expensive war films at the time of its release.
Pearl Harbor used 24 vintage aircraft for its action sequences, the largest collection of flyable World War II planes assembled for a film.
Inglourious Basterds (2009) is Quentin Tarantino's revisionist World War II film in which a group of Jewish-American soldiers hunt Nazis in occupied France.
Christoph Waltz won the Academy Award for Best Supporting Actor for his portrayal of SS Colonel Hans Landa in Inglourious Basterds, a role Tarantino called the best character he ever wrote.
Tarantino reportedly considered canceling Inglourious Basterds entirely because he could not find the right actor for Hans Landa until Christoph Waltz auditioned.
The opening farmhouse scene of Inglourious Basterds, lasting roughly 20 minutes, is frequently cited as one of the most tense dialogue sequences in modern cinema.
Dunkirk (2017) had only about 60 pages of dialogue in its screenplay, far fewer than the typical 120 pages, reflecting Nolan's emphasis on visual storytelling.
Paths of Glory (1957), directed by Stanley Kubrick, is a powerful anti-war film about French soldiers in World War I court-martialed for cowardice.
Paths of Glory was banned in France for nearly 20 years because of its critical depiction of the French military.
Kirk Douglas starred in Paths of Glory as Colonel Dax, a role that solidified his reputation as a leading actor willing to tackle controversial material.
Dr. Strangelove (1964), Kubrick's Cold War satire, features Peter Sellers playing three different roles: Group Captain Mandrake, President Muffley, and Dr. Strangelove himself.
Peter Sellers was originally supposed to play a fourth role in Dr. Strangelove—Major Kong—but a broken leg limited his mobility, so Slim Pickens took the part.
Slim Pickens was never shown the full script of Dr. Strangelove and played Major Kong's bomb-riding scene completely straight, not realizing the film was a comedy.
The war room set in Dr. Strangelove was designed by Ken Adam, who also designed many of the iconic James Bond sets.
M*A*S*H (1970) was Robert Altman's black comedy set during the Korean War that spawned the legendary TV series lasting 11 seasons.
The finale of the M*A*S*H TV series in 1983 was watched by an estimated 106 million viewers, making it the most-watched broadcast in American television history for decades.
Apocalypse Now took nearly three years to complete, from its start in the Philippines in March 1976 to its premiere at Cannes in May 1979.
Francis Ford Coppola mortgaged his home and vineyard to finance parts of Apocalypse Now when the budget spiraled out of control.
The documentary Hearts of Darkness: A Filmmaker's Apocalypse (1991) chronicles the chaotic making of Apocalypse Now, filmed by Eleanor Coppola, Francis's wife.
Valkyrie (2008) starred Tom Cruise as Colonel Claus von Stauffenberg, the German officer who attempted to assassinate Adolf Hitler in July 1944.
The real Operation Valkyrie on July 20, 1944, came within minutes of succeeding; the briefcase bomb detonated but Hitler survived because a heavy oak table leg deflected the blast.
Enemy at the Gates (2001) depicts the Battle of Stalingrad and the legendary sniper duel between Soviet sniper Vasily Zaitsev and a German marksman.
Historians debate whether the sniper duel depicted in Enemy at the Gates actually occurred, as evidence for a German sniper named Major König is thin.
We Were Soldiers (2002) depicts the Battle of Ia Drang in 1965, one of the first major engagements between U.S. and North Vietnamese forces.
Mel Gibson studied extensively with Lt. Gen. Hal Moore, whose book We Were Soldiers Once... and Young formed the basis of the film.
Windtalkers (2002) told the story of Navajo Code Talkers who used their native language to transmit coded messages during World War II.
The Navajo code used in World War II was never broken by the Japanese and is the only spoken military code never to have been deciphered.
Twelve O'Clock High (1949) starring Gregory Peck is one of the most influential war films about aerial combat, depicting the psychological toll on bomber crews.
Twelve O'Clock High was used as a leadership training film by military academies for decades after its release.
The Dirty Dozen (1967) was a massive box office hit about a group of convicted soldiers trained for a suicide mission behind enemy lines.
The Dirty Dozen featured Lee Marvin, Charles Bronson, Jim Brown, John Cassavetes, and Telly Savalas in one of Hollywood's great ensemble casts.
Where Eagles Dare (1968) starred Richard Burton and Clint Eastwood in a commando mission to rescue a general from a mountaintop Nazi fortress.
Clint Eastwood's role in Where Eagles Dare was one of his earliest major Hollywood productions before he became a star director.
A Bridge Too Far (1977) depicted the disastrous Operation Market Garden of 1944, featuring an all-star cast including Sean Connery, Robert Redford, and Anthony Hopkins.
Operation Market Garden, depicted in A Bridge Too Far, was the largest airborne operation in history, involving over 35,000 troops.
Gallipoli (1981) directed by Peter Weir follows Australian soldiers during the 1915 Gallipoli Campaign and helped launch Mel Gibson's international career.
The Battle of Gallipoli depicted in the film resulted in over 250,000 casualties on each side and is commemorated as ANZAC Day in Australia and New Zealand.
Empire of the Sun (1987) was Steven Spielberg's film about a young British boy (Christian Bale) surviving a Japanese internment camp in Shanghai during WWII.
Empire of the Sun was a 13-year-old Christian Bale's first major film role, launching a career that would span decades.
The Killing Fields (1984) depicted the Cambodian genocide under the Khmer Rouge, with Haing S. Ngor winning the Best Supporting Actor Oscar despite having no prior acting experience.
Haing S. Ngor was himself a survivor of the Cambodian genocide, lending his performance in The Killing Fields devastating authenticity.
Born on the Fourth of July (1989) starred Tom Cruise as paralyzed Vietnam veteran Ron Kovic and earned Oliver Stone his second Best Director Oscar.
Tom Cruise spent months in a wheelchair to prepare for Born on the Fourth of July, visiting VA hospitals and meeting with paralyzed veterans.
Heaven & Earth (1993) completed Oliver Stone's Vietnam War trilogy, following Born on the Fourth of July and Platoon.
Oliver Stone is the only major director to have made three significant Vietnam War films, all drawing from different perspectives of the conflict.
Hamburger Hill (1987) depicted the brutal 10-day assault on Hill 937 during the Vietnam War, which cost 72 American lives to capture a position that was abandoned shortly after.
The Battle of Hamburger Hill was so controversial that it contributed to a shift in U.S. military strategy in Vietnam, moving away from large-scale frontal assaults.
Casualties of War (1989) was based on a true incident in which American soldiers in Vietnam kidnapped and murdered a Vietnamese civilian.
Tom Hanks has starred in multiple war films, including Saving Private Ryan, Road to Perdition, Captain Phillips, and Bridge of Spies.
Tom Hanks co-produced Band of Brothers, The Pacific, and Masters of the Air, creating a trilogy of World War II miniseries spanning nearly two decades.
The Pacific (2010) was the companion miniseries to Band of Brothers, following Marines in the Pacific Theater of World War II.
Masters of the Air (2024) completed the World War II miniseries trilogy produced by Tom Hanks and Steven Spielberg, focusing on the Eighth Air Force's bombing campaign over Europe.
Steven Spielberg has directed more critically acclaimed war films than any other living director, including Schindler's List, Saving Private Ryan, War Horse, and Bridge of Spies.
Spielberg's interest in World War II was reportedly sparked by his father's service in the U.S. Army Air Corps during the war.
Stanley Kubrick directed three films dealing with war and the military: Paths of Glory, Dr. Strangelove, and Full Metal Jacket.
Kubrick was famously meticulous—for Full Metal Jacket, he required that every palm tree be individually approved before it was planted on the set in England.
Francis Ford Coppola's Apocalypse Now is frequently ranked among the greatest films ever made, not just the greatest war films.
Christopher Nolan approached Dunkirk as more of a survival thriller than a traditional war film, choosing to avoid graphic gore.
Oliver Stone served in the U.S. Army in Vietnam and was wounded twice in combat, earning a Bronze Star and a Purple Heart.
Clint Eastwood has both directed and starred in war films, including Heartbreak Ridge, Flags of Our Fathers, and Letters from Iwo Jima.
Clint Eastwood was drafted into the U.S. Army during the Korean War but served as a swimming instructor at Fort Ord in California rather than seeing combat.
Sam Fuller, director of The Big Red One (1980), served in the 1st Infantry Division during World War II and drew directly from his combat experiences.
Samuel Fuller carried a cigar and a camera through North Africa, Sicily, D-Day at Omaha Beach, and the liberation of a concentration camp.
The Big Red One (1980) starring Lee Marvin was Fuller's autobiographical account of his service from North Africa to the liberation of a concentration camp.
Terrence Malick spent 20 years away from filmmaking before returning to direct The Thin Red Line in 1998.
Denis Villeneuve's Incendies (2010), while not a traditional war film, depicted the Lebanese Civil War and earned an Academy Award nomination for Best Foreign Language Film.
Sam Mendes directed 1917 and also directed the James Bond films Skyfall and Spectre.
Saving Private Ryan lost the Best Picture Oscar to Shakespeare in Love in 1999, a result considered one of the biggest upsets in Academy Award history.
Tom Hanks was not nominated for Best Actor for Saving Private Ryan, surprising many critics who considered it one of his finest performances.
The Academy Award for Best Picture has gone to more war films than almost any other genre, with winners including Patton, Platoon, Schindler's List, and The Hurt Locker.
Platoon was the first Vietnam War film to win the Academy Award for Best Picture.
Apocalypse Now was nominated for eight Academy Awards and won two: Best Cinematography and Best Sound.
The Hurt Locker won six Academy Awards from nine nominations at the 82nd Academy Awards ceremony in 2010.
Dunkirk received eight Oscar nominations and won three: Best Film Editing, Best Sound Editing, and Best Sound Mixing.
1917 won three Academy Awards: Best Cinematography, Best Visual Effects, and Best Sound Mixing.
All Quiet on the Western Front (2022) became only the third film in a language other than English to be nominated for the Best Picture Oscar.
Sergeant York (1941) starred Gary Cooper as the real-life World War I hero Alvin York, who captured 132 German soldiers almost single-handedly.
Gary Cooper won the Academy Award for Best Actor for Sergeant York, one of the most popular war films of the World War II era.
The real Alvin York initially refused to allow a film about his life until he was promised the proceeds would fund a Bible school in his hometown.
From Here to Eternity (1953), set at an Army base in Hawaii just before the attack on Pearl Harbor, won eight Academy Awards including Best Picture.
The famous beach scene between Burt Lancaster and Deborah Kerr in From Here to Eternity is one of the most iconic romantic images in film history.
Stalag 17 (1953), directed by Billy Wilder, featured William Holden as a cynical American POW suspected of being a German spy.
William Holden won the Academy Award for Best Actor for Stalag 17, beating Burt Lancaster and Montgomery Clift.
The Guns of Navarone (1961) starred Gregory Peck, David Niven, and Anthony Quinn in a mission to destroy massive German guns on a Greek island.
Apocalypse Now's production budget ballooned from an initial estimate of $12 million to a final cost of approximately $31 million—over $120 million in today's money.
The Vietnam War film genre emerged primarily in the late 1970s, nearly a decade after American withdrawal, because studios were initially reluctant to confront the conflict.
Saving Private Ryan earned over $480 million worldwide against a $70 million budget, making it one of the most commercially successful war films ever.
Steven Spielberg turned down the opportunity to direct Schindler's List several times because he felt he was not mature enough as a filmmaker to handle the subject matter.
Military advisor Dale Dye has worked on over 30 war films, including Platoon, Saving Private Ryan, Band of Brothers, and The Pacific.
Dale Dye founded Warriors, Inc., a company dedicated to training actors in realistic military procedures for film and television.
Many war films employ real military veterans as extras and technical advisors to ensure authentic portrayal of combat tactics and military culture.
For Saving Private Ryan, military advisors taught the cast how to properly handle M1 Garand rifles, Thompson submachine guns, and BAR rifles from the 1940s.
The explosions on the beach in Saving Private Ryan used real amputees wearing prosthetic limbs that were blown off with special effects charges for horrifying realism.
Hacksaw Ridge used military advisors who specialized in World War II Pacific Theater tactics to choreograph the battle sequences on Okinawa.
The tank battles in Fury were choreographed with the help of historians from the Bovington Tank Museum to ensure accurate World War II armored tactics.
In Black Hawk Down, technical advisors included actual Delta Force operators and Rangers who had participated in the real Battle of Mogadishu.
War films often alter historical facts for dramatic purposes; The Bridge on the River Kwai fabricated the bridge's destruction, which never actually happened.
Saving Private Ryan compressed the timeline of the Normandy campaign and invented the central rescue mission, though the Niland brothers' story was real.
Dunkirk accurately depicted the broad strokes of the evacuation but invented its specific characters and combined events from multiple days.
The Imitation Game (2014), while primarily a biopic about Alan Turing, depicts the code-breaking efforts at Bletchley Park that were crucial to the Allied war effort.
U-571 (2000) controversially depicted Americans capturing a German Enigma machine, when in reality British sailors accomplished the feat.
The British Parliament criticized U-571 for rewriting history, and Tony Blair called the film an affront to real British sailors.
Letters from Iwo Jima was praised for its historically accurate portrayal of the Japanese defensive strategy at Iwo Jima under General Kuribayashi.
Many Vietnam War films including Platoon, Apocalypse Now, and Casualties of War were filmed in the Philippines rather than Vietnam.
Full Metal Jacket's depiction of Marine boot camp is considered so accurate that it has been used as a reference point for understanding military indoctrination.
The sand beaches at Curracloe, County Wexford, Ireland, doubled for Omaha Beach in the filming of Saving Private Ryan.
Saving Private Ryan was released on July 24, 1998, and became the highest-grossing film of the year in the United States.
War films experienced a resurgence in the late 1990s following the success of Saving Private Ryan and The Thin Red Line in 1998.
Platoon was released on December 19, 1986, and became the first film from a Vietnam veteran to win the Best Picture Oscar.
Apocalypse Now premiered at the Cannes Film Festival on May 19, 1979, in a rough work-in-progress form that Coppola was still editing.
The Deer Hunter premiered at the 1978 Berlin International Film Festival, sparking immediate controversy and acclaim.
Schindler's List was released on December 15, 1993, during the holiday season—an unusual release date for a film about the Holocaust.
Black Hawk Down was released on December 28, 2001, less than four months after the September 11 attacks, giving the film added resonance with American audiences.
American Sniper was released on Christmas Day 2014 in limited release before going wide in January 2015.
1917 was released on December 25, 2019, in a limited release to qualify for Academy Awards before expanding nationwide in January 2020.
Hacksaw Ridge was released on November 4, 2016, opening to strong reviews and eventually earning over $175 million worldwide.
Dunkirk was released on July 21, 2017, during the summer blockbuster season—unusual for a war film—and earned over $526 million worldwide.
War films have collectively earned billions at the worldwide box office; Saving Private Ryan, Dunkirk, and American Sniper each grossed over $350 million.
American Sniper's domestic gross of $350 million made it the highest-grossing film of 2014 in the United States.
Pearl Harbor (2001) grossed $449 million worldwide despite largely negative reviews from critics.
Dunkirk earned $526 million worldwide, making it Christopher Nolan's fourth-highest-grossing film at the time.
Hacksaw Ridge earned $175 million worldwide against a $40 million budget, making it a significant financial success.
The Hurt Locker earned only $49 million worldwide—the lowest box office gross for a Best Picture winner in modern history.
Inglourious Basterds earned $321 million worldwide, making it Quentin Tarantino's highest-grossing film at the time of its release.
Zero Dark Thirty earned $132 million worldwide and received five Academy Award nominations.
The 2022 All Quiet on the Western Front cost approximately $20 million to produce and was released primarily through Netflix.
War documentaries have also achieved critical acclaim; The Fog of War (2003), featuring former Secretary of Defense Robert McNamara, won the Academy Award for Best Documentary.
They Shall Not Grow Old (2018) was Peter Jackson's documentary that used cutting-edge technology to colorize and restore World War I footage.
Peter Jackson's team colorized and added sound effects and lip-read dialogue to 100-year-old World War I footage for They Shall Not Grow Old.
Ken Burns's documentary The War (2007) is a 14-hour examination of World War II told through the stories of people from four American towns.
Restrepo (2010) is a documentary about a platoon of U.S. soldiers in Afghanistan's Korengal Valley, one of the most dangerous postings in the war.
Sebastian Junger and Tim Hetherington co-directed Restrepo while embedded with the soldiers; Hetherington was later killed covering the Libyan Civil War.
The Vietnam War (2017), Ken Burns and Lynn Novick's 18-hour documentary, took over 10 years to produce and featured testimony from soldiers on both sides of the conflict.
Shoah (1985) by Claude Lanzmann is a nine-and-a-half-hour documentary about the Holocaust that uses no archival footage, relying entirely on interviews and present-day locations.
The World at War (1973-74) is a 26-episode British documentary series narrated by Laurence Olivier that remains one of the definitive audiovisual histories of World War II.
Five Came Back (2017) is a Netflix documentary series about five Hollywood directors—John Ford, William Wyler, John Huston, Frank Capra, and George Stevens—who served in World War II.
John Ford directed the documentary The Battle of Midway (1942), filming actual combat footage during the battle while being wounded by shrapnel.
William Wyler flew on actual combat missions over Germany to film Memphis Belle: A Story of a Flying Fortress (1944), losing his hearing from the noise.
John Huston directed The Battle of San Pietro (1945), a documentary about the Italian Campaign that was initially suppressed by the Army for being too realistic about combat.
George Stevens documented the liberation of Dachau concentration camp with his camera; the footage was later used as evidence at the Nuremberg trials.
Frank Capra produced the Why We Fight series of seven propaganda films for the U.S. government during World War II.
Catch-22 (1970) was Mike Nichols's adaptation of Joseph Heller's satirical anti-war novel set during World War II.
Joseph Heller's novel Catch-22 coined the term 'catch-22' to describe a paradoxical situation with no escape, which has since entered everyday language.
The Grand Illusion (1937), directed by Jean Renoir, is considered one of the greatest anti-war films ever made, depicting French POWs in World War I.
The Grand Illusion was the first foreign-language film to be nominated for the Academy Award for Best Picture.

Grave of the Fireflies (1988) is a Japanese animated film about two children struggling to survive in the final months of World War II, considered one of the most devastating anti-war films ever made.
Grave of the Fireflies was produced by Studio Ghibli and released as a double feature with My Neighbor Totoro, creating a jarring contrast between the two films.
Johnny Got His Gun (1971), directed by Dalton Trumbo based on his own novel, follows a World War I soldier who has lost his limbs, sight, hearing, and speech.
Dalton Trumbo wrote the anti-war novel Johnny Got His Gun while blacklisted during the McCarthy era.
The anti-war theme is central to many of the most acclaimed war films, including All Quiet on the Western Front, Paths of Glory, and Apocalypse Now.
Kubrick's anti-war stance was so strong that Paths of Glory was banned in France, Switzerland, and some U.S. military bases.
Bridge of Spies (2015) starred Tom Hanks as a lawyer defending a Soviet spy during the Cold War, directed by Steven Spielberg with a script co-written by the Coen Brothers.
Mark Rylance won the Best Supporting Actor Oscar for Bridge of Spies playing Soviet spy Rudolf Abel with quiet, understated dignity.
Troy (2004), while set in ancient times, employed massive battle sequences and practical effects that influenced modern war filmmaking.
Braveheart (1995) won five Academy Awards, including Best Picture and Best Director for Mel Gibson, despite significant historical inaccuracies.
The battle scenes in Braveheart were filmed using 1,600 extras from the Irish Army Reserve.
Gladiator (2000) revived the historical war epic genre and won five Academy Awards, including Best Picture and Best Actor for Russell Crowe.
Beasts of No Nation (2015) was a Netflix original film starring Idris Elba as a warlord who conscripts child soldiers in an unnamed African conflict.
Beasts of No Nation was one of the first major Netflix original films, but its day-and-date release on the platform caused major theater chains to boycott it.
Lebanon (2009), an Israeli war film set entirely inside a tank during the 1982 Lebanon War, won the Golden Lion at the Venice Film Festival.
Waltz with Bashir (2008) is an animated documentary about the 1982 Lebanon War, using animation to explore the filmmaker's suppressed memories of the Sabra and Shatila massacre.
The Pianist, The Deer Hunter, and Platoon all won multiple Academy Awards, cementing their directors as masters of the war genre.
The first war film ever made is generally considered to be Tearing Down the Spanish Flag (1898), a short film made during the Spanish-American War.
Wings (1927), a silent film about World War I fighter pilots, won the first-ever Academy Award for Best Picture.
The helicopter sequences in Apocalypse Now pioneered techniques for aerial filming that are still used in war movies today.
Modern war films increasingly use digital effects combined with practical explosions—a technique perfected in films like Black Hawk Down and Hacksaw Ridge.
IMAX cameras were first used extensively in a war film for Dunkirk, setting a new standard for visual immersion in the genre.
Sound design in war films has become increasingly sophisticated; Saving Private Ryan won the Academy Award for Best Sound Effects Editing for its immersive battlefield audio.
The sound team for Saving Private Ryan recorded actual vintage weaponry and explosions to create an authentic World War II soundscape.
Gary Rydstrom, the sound designer for Saving Private Ryan, said the opening sequence was designed so that audiences would feel like they were 'inside the battle.'
Hans Zimmer's score for Dunkirk incorporated the sound of a ticking pocket watch, recorded from Christopher Nolan's own watch, to build tension throughout the film.
Many war films use the 'Shepard tone' — an audio illusion of a continuously rising pitch — to create unrelenting tension, a technique used prominently in Dunkirk.
The three-strip Technicolor process was first used in war filmmaking during World War II documentaries, giving audiences unprecedentedly vivid images of combat.
John Williams composed the score for Saving Private Ryan but deliberately used minimal music during the battle scenes to preserve the raw realism of combat.
John Williams also composed the score for Schindler's List, with Itzhak Perlman performing the haunting violin solos.
Ennio Morricone, famous for his Western scores, also composed music for several war films including The Good, the Bad and the Ugly, which has war as a major backdrop.
Samuel Barber's Adagio for Strings has been used in multiple war films, most notably in Platoon, where it accompanies some of the film's most emotional scenes.
The Wagner helicopter attack scene in Apocalypse Now directly influenced the 'Ride of the Valkyries' becoming a pop-culture symbol of military aggression.
Clint Eastwood composed the music for both Flags of Our Fathers and Letters from Iwo Jima himself, in addition to directing both films.
War films frequently sweep the technical categories at the Oscars, particularly Sound Design, Film Editing, and Visual Effects.
The term 'war film' encompasses multiple sub-genres including prisoner-of-war dramas, submarine films, aerial combat films, anti-war satires, and home front stories.
Prisoner-of-war films like The Great Escape, Stalag 17, and The Bridge on the River Kwai form their own distinct sub-genre within war cinema.
Submarine war films including Das Boot, U-571, The Hunt for Red October, and Crimson Tide exploit claustrophobia and psychological tension unique to underwater combat.
Aerial combat films such as Top Gun, Memphis Belle, and Red Tails focus on fighter and bomber crews, emphasizing the isolation and danger of air warfare.
The Red Tails (2012) depicted the Tuskegee Airmen, the first African-American military aviators in the U.S. Armed Forces during World War II.
Tuskegee Airmen flew over 15,000 combat sorties and never lost a single bomber they escorted to enemy fighters — a claim often cited though debated by historians.
Memphis Belle (1990) told the story of the first B-17 crew to complete 25 missions over Europe, becoming eligible to return home.
The Memphis Belle aircraft flew its 25th and final mission on May 17, 1943, and was then sent home for a war bonds tour across the United States.
The real Memphis Belle is now on display at the National Museum of the United States Air Force in Dayton, Ohio, after a lengthy restoration.
Unbroken (2014), directed by Angelina Jolie, follows Olympic athlete Louis Zamperini's survival as a POW in Japanese camps during World War II.
Louis Zamperini survived 47 days on a life raft in the Pacific and then two years in brutal Japanese POW camps before being liberated.
The Railway Man (2013) starred Colin Firth as a British soldier who was forced to work on the Burma Railway and later confronted his Japanese torturer.
Merry Christmas, Mr. Lawrence (1983) starred David Bowie as a British prisoner of war in a Japanese camp, with a score composed by Ryuichi Sakamoto.
The Great Raid (2005) depicted the Raid at Cabanatuan in 1945, when U.S. Army Rangers rescued over 500 Allied POWs from a Japanese camp in the Philippines.
The Raid at Cabanatuan is considered one of the most successful rescue missions in military history.
Three Kings (1999) directed by David O. Russell was a dark comedy set at the end of the Gulf War about soldiers who discover a map leading to stolen Kuwaiti gold.
Jarhead (2005), based on Anthony Swofford's memoir, depicted the psychological strain of Marines waiting for combat during the Gulf War rather than actual fighting.
The Siege of Jadotville (2016) is an Irish film about 150 UN peacekeepers who held off 3,000 attackers in the Congo in 1961, a battle largely forgotten by history.
Dunkirk's cast included several young and relatively unknown actors, a deliberate choice by Nolan to make the soldiers feel anonymous and vulnerable.
Harry Styles of One Direction made his major acting debut in Dunkirk, earning praise from critics for his understated performance.
Audie Murphy, the most decorated American soldier of World War II, starred as himself in To Hell and Back (1955), reenacting his own combat experiences.
Audie Murphy earned every military combat award available from the U.S. Army, including the Medal of Honor, before turning 21.
Jimmy Stewart served as a bomber pilot during World War II, flying 20 combat missions over Germany and rising to the rank of brigadier general in the Air Force Reserve.
Clark Gable enlisted in the Army Air Corps after his wife Carole Lombard was killed in a plane crash during a war bonds tour and flew combat missions over Germany.
Lee Marvin was a Marine who was wounded at the Battle of Saipan in 1944, spending over a year in recovery before returning to civilian life and acting.
Charles Durning, who appeared in many war films, landed on Omaha Beach on D-Day and was one of only three men in his unit to survive the initial assault.
Oliver Stone joined the U.S. Army voluntarily in 1967 and requested combat duty in Vietnam, where he was wounded twice.
Samuel Fuller landed at Omaha Beach, fought through France, and liberated the Falkenau concentration camp — all experiences that directly informed his war films.
John Ford was awarded the Purple Heart after being wounded while filming the Battle of Midway in 1942.
War films are among the most honored at international film festivals; Apocalypse Now, The Thin Red Line, and Letters from Iwo Jima all competed for the Palme d'Or at Cannes.
The Venice Film Festival has honored war films including Lebanon (2009) and Joker (2019), the latter drawing on themes of societal violence.
War films have successfully launched franchises; the TV series M*A*S*H ran for 11 seasons after the 1970 film, and Band of Brothers spawned two follow-up miniseries.
The phrase 'I love the smell of napalm in the morning' from Apocalypse Now, delivered by Robert Duvall, is one of the most quoted lines in cinema history.
Apocalypse Now's famous line was improvised by Robert Duvall, who remained in character as the surf-obsessed Lieutenant Colonel Kilgore throughout filming.
The line 'Earn this' whispered by Captain Miller (Tom Hanks) at the end of Saving Private Ryan became a defining phrase for the film's moral message.
In Patton, the famous opening speech was assembled from multiple speeches Patton gave to different units, condensed into one electrifying monologue.
Full Metal Jacket's iconic line 'This is my rifle. There are many like it, but this one is mine' is taken directly from the U.S. Marines' Rifleman's Creed.
The phrase 'Charlie don't surf' from Apocalypse Now was later adopted as a band name and became part of pop culture lexicon.
In war filmmaking, the 'fog of war' is both a thematic element and a practical challenge; directors deliberately create chaos in battle scenes to replicate the confusion of combat.
Cross of Iron (1977), directed by Sam Peckinpah, was one of the few major Western war films to tell its story from the German perspective during World War II.
Sam Peckinpah's Cross of Iron influenced later filmmakers including Quentin Tarantino, who called it one of the best war films ever made.
Kelly's Heroes (1970) starred Clint Eastwood in a comedic war film about American soldiers who go AWOL to steal Nazi gold.
The Manchurian Candidate (1962), while technically a Cold War thriller, explored brainwashing of POWs and was considered so politically sensitive that it was pulled from distribution for years.
The Manchurian Candidate was pulled from theaters after JFK's assassination and not re-released until 1988.
Good Morning, Vietnam was released on December 23, 1987, during the holiday season and became one of Robin Williams's biggest box office hits.
The Boys in Company C (1978) was one of the earliest Vietnam War films, predating Apocalypse Now and The Deer Hunter by about a year.
Go Tell the Spartans (1978) starring Burt Lancaster was set in the early stages of American involvement in Vietnam in 1964.
The Green Berets (1968) starring John Wayne is the only major Hollywood Vietnam War film made during the conflict itself, presenting a strongly pro-war perspective.
John Wayne's The Green Berets was widely criticized by anti-war commentators but was a commercial success, reflecting the divided American public.
Rescue Dawn (2006), directed by Werner Herzog, told the true story of Dieter Dengler, a U.S. Navy pilot who escaped from a Laotian POW camp during the Vietnam War.
Christian Bale ate only minimal food to lose significant weight for Rescue Dawn, just as he had done for The Machinist.
Land of Mine (2015), a Danish film, depicted young German POWs forced to clear over two million landmines from Danish beaches after World War II.
After World War II, an estimated 2,000 German POWs were forced to remove roughly 1.4 million mines from Danish beaches, with many killed or maimed.
Son of Saul (2015), a Hungarian film set in Auschwitz, won the Academy Award for Best Foreign Language Film and the Grand Prix at Cannes.
Son of Saul was shot almost entirely in close-up, keeping the horrors of the concentration camp in a deliberate, haunting blur in the background.
The Battle of Algiers (1966), an Italian-Algerian co-production about the Algerian War of Independence, was so realistic that the film carried a disclaimer stating no documentary footage was used.
The Battle of Algiers was reportedly screened by the Pentagon in 2003 to understand the challenges of urban guerrilla warfare before the Iraq War.
Ivan's Childhood (1962), Andrei Tarkovsky's debut feature, follows a young Soviet boy who serves as a scout during World War II.
The Cranes Are Flying (1957), a Soviet film, won the Palme d'Or at Cannes and is considered one of the greatest Russian war films.
War films from non-Western perspectives have gained increasing recognition, including City of Life and Death (2009) about the Nanjing Massacre and Tae Guk Gi (2004) about the Korean War.
Tae Guk Gi (2004), a South Korean film about two brothers fighting in the Korean War, was the highest-grossing Korean film at the time of its release.
Clint Eastwood was 76 years old when he directed Letters from Iwo Jima and Flags of Our Fathers, demonstrating his remarkable productivity in his later career.
Steven Spielberg originally offered the director's chair for Schindler's List to Roman Polanski, Sydney Pollack, Billy Wilder, and Martin Scorsese before deciding to direct it himself.
Martin Scorsese returned the favor by taking over Spielberg's project Cape Fear while Spielberg committed to making Schindler's List.
War films often require massive sets; the production of Saving Private Ryan recreated portions of Omaha Beach using over 1,500 extras and real military landing craft.
The production of 1917 required the construction of over 5,200 feet of trenches in a continuous path that the camera could follow.
Hacksaw Ridge's battle sequences were filmed over a period of several weeks, with stunt coordinators creating explosions using a combination of practical effects and CGI.
The production of Apocalypse Now used over 1,200 extras and 1,500 gallons of gasoline for the famous napalm strike scene alone.
War films based on true stories often face challenges balancing historical accuracy with dramatic storytelling, leading to debates among historians and veterans.
The Department of Defense has a branch called the Entertainment Liaison Office that works with Hollywood productions to ensure accurate portrayals of the military.
To receive DoD cooperation including military equipment and personnel, filmmakers must submit their scripts for review and sometimes agree to changes.
Films that portray the military negatively—like Apocalypse Now, Platoon, and The Hurt Locker—typically do not receive Department of Defense support.
Saving Private Ryan did not receive Department of Defense support because the Army objected to the mission's premise of risking several lives to save one soldier.
Black Hawk Down received extensive military cooperation including actual Black Hawk helicopters and military personnel as extras.
Top Gun (1986), while an action film, dramatized aerial combat and led to a significant increase in Navy recruitment.
Top Gun: Maverick (2022) became the highest-grossing film of Tom Cruise's career, earning over $1.4 billion worldwide.
The adaptation of war literature to film is a long tradition; All Quiet on the Western Front, Catch-22, and The Thin Red Line all originated as acclaimed novels.
Tim O'Brien's The Things They Carried, widely considered the definitive literary work about the Vietnam War, has been in development for film adaptation for decades.
Joseph Heller served as a bombardier in World War II, and his experiences directly inspired the absurdist novel Catch-22.
Kurt Vonnegut's Slaughterhouse-Five was adapted into a 1972 film; Vonnegut was himself a POW in Dresden during its infamous firebombing.
The war film genre has evolved significantly: early films often glorified combat, while post-Vietnam films increasingly explored the futility and psychological toll of war.
Pre-World War II war films like Sergeant York and The Fighting 69th were often used as morale-boosting propaganda.
Post-Vietnam war cinema shifted toward more cynical portrayals. Films like Platoon and Full Metal Jacket showed war as morally ambiguous and psychologically devastating.
Modern war films about Iraq and Afghanistan—including The Hurt Locker, American Sniper, and Restrepo—focus heavily on the personal impact of combat on individual soldiers.
The psychological effects of war depicted in films like The Deer Hunter and Born on the Fourth of July helped raise public awareness about PTSD.
Oliver Stone has said that without the Vietnam War films of the 1970s and 80s, the American public might never have fully reckoned with the true cost of the conflict.
Jojo Rabbit (2019), directed by Taika Waititi, was a satirical comedy about a young Hitler Youth member whose imaginary friend is a bumbling Adolf Hitler.
Taika Waititi, who is of Maori and Jewish descent, played Adolf Hitler in Jojo Rabbit and won the Best Adapted Screenplay Oscar.
The Life Is Beautiful (1997), an Italian film by Roberto Benigni, won three Academy Awards for its tragicomic portrayal of a father protecting his son in a concentration camp.
Roberto Benigni famously climbed over theater seats to reach the stage when he won the Best Actor Oscar for Life Is Beautiful.
The Counterfeiters (2007), an Austrian film about Jewish prisoners forced to forge foreign currency in a concentration camp, won the Academy Award for Best Foreign Language Film.
Defiance (2008) starred Daniel Craig as one of the Bielski partisans, Jewish brothers who saved over 1,200 Jews from the Nazis by hiding them in the forests of Belarus.
The Monuments Men (2014) directed by George Clooney told the story of Allied soldiers tasked with recovering art stolen by the Nazis.
During World War II, the real Monuments Men recovered over five million works of art, cultural objects, and treasures that the Nazis had looted from across Europe.
Midway (2019) depicted the Battle of Midway in 1942, considered the turning point of the Pacific War.
The Battle of Midway occurred from June 4-7, 1942, resulting in the destruction of four Japanese aircraft carriers.
Greyhound (2020) starred Tom Hanks as a U.S. Navy commander escorting a convoy across the Atlantic during the Battle of the Atlantic. Hanks also wrote the screenplay.
13 Hours: The Secret Soldiers of Benghazi (2016), directed by Michael Bay, depicted the 2012 attack on a U.S. diplomatic compound in Libya.
Eye in the Sky (2015) explored the ethical dilemmas of drone warfare, featuring Helen Mirren as a military officer coordinating a drone strike from thousands of miles away.
Drone warfare films like Eye in the Sky and Good Kill (2014) represent a new sub-genre reflecting the changing nature of modern combat.
War films that address the home front—such as Mrs. Miniver (1942) and The Best Years of Our Lives (1946)—explore war's impact on civilians and returning veterans.
The Best Years of Our Lives (1946) won seven Academy Awards and featured Harold Russell, a real veteran who lost both hands, winning two Oscars for his performance.
Harold Russell remains the only actor to win two Academy Awards for the same role—Best Supporting Actor and an honorary award for bringing hope to other veterans.
Mrs. Miniver (1942) won six Academy Awards including Best Picture and was credited by Winston Churchill with doing more for the war effort than a flotilla of destroyers.
Casablanca (1942), set against the backdrop of World War II, won the Best Picture Oscar and is considered one of the greatest films ever made.
The Counterfeit Traitor (1962) starred William Holden as a Swedish businessman who becomes a spy for the Allies during World War II.
Overlord (2018) was a war-horror hybrid set during D-Day in which American paratroopers discover Nazi experiments creating zombie-like super soldiers.
The war-horror hybrid is a small but growing sub-genre that includes films like Overlord, Outpost (2008), and Frankenstein's Army (2013).
War comedies represent another sub-genre, including M*A*S*H, Kelly's Heroes, The Dirty Dozen, and more recently Jojo Rabbit.
The 317th Platoon (1965), a French film about the First Indochina War, is considered one of the most authentic combat films ever made.
Fires on the Plain (1959), a Japanese film about starving soldiers in the Philippines, is one of the most unflinching anti-war films ever produced.
Germany Year Zero (1948), Roberto Rossellini's neorealist film about a boy in post-war Berlin, is a haunting depiction of war's aftermath on innocent civilians.
Roberto Rossellini's War Trilogy—Rome Open City, Paisà, and Germany Year Zero—pioneered Italian neorealism while documenting the devastation of World War II in Europe.
The war film genre has produced more Best Picture Oscar winners than any other genre, reflecting the Academy's consistent recognition of films about armed conflict.
Danger Close: The Battle of Long Tan (2019) depicted the 1966 battle in which 108 Australian soldiers fought off over 2,000 Viet Cong.
Beneath Hill 60 (2010) told the story of Australian miners who tunneled under German lines during World War I to plant explosives.
The Battle of the Somme (1916) is a British documentary that used real and staged footage and was seen by an estimated 20 million people in Britain within six weeks of release.
War films continue to evolve with technology; future productions will likely make greater use of virtual production stages and AI-enhanced visual effects.
Saving Private Ryan's influence on subsequent war films is immeasurable; its handheld camera work, desaturated color palette, and visceral sound design became the gold standard.
Many war film directors have cited Saving Private Ryan as the film that changed how combat is depicted on screen.
The phrase 'Spielberg face'—a close-up of a character in awe—appears in many of his war films, including Schindler's List and Saving Private Ryan.
The number of war films produced worldwide has increased significantly since the 2000s, with countries like South Korea, China, and Turkey producing major war epics.
The 800 (2020), a Chinese film about the defense of Shanghai's Sihang Warehouse in 1937, became the highest-grossing film worldwide that year during the pandemic.
The Water Diviner (2014), Russell Crowe's directorial debut, depicted the Battle of Gallipoli and its aftermath from both Australian and Turkish perspectives.
Beneath the sand on many of the beaches where D-Day was reenacted for films, real unexploded ordnance from 1944 still occasionally surfaces.
In the shadow of war films' entertainment value lies their educational role; studies have shown that many people learn about historical conflicts primarily through cinema.
Anthropoid (2016) depicted Operation Anthropoid, the assassination of SS leader Reinhard Heydrich in Prague in 1942.
Operation Anthropoid was the only successful assassination of a high-ranking Nazi official during World War II.
The forgotten history of war's child soldiers has been explored in films like Beasts of No Nation and The Boy in the Striped Pajamas (2008).
The Boy in the Striped Pajamas (2008) tells the friendship between the son of a concentration camp commandant and a Jewish boy inside the camp.
Darkest Hour (2017) featured Gary Oldman in a transformative performance as Winston Churchill during the early days of World War II, winning him the Best Actor Oscar.
Gary Oldman spent over 200 hours in makeup to transform into Winston Churchill for Darkest Hour, with prosthetics applied by Kazuhiro Tsuji.
Guy Hamilton, who directed The Battle of Britain (1969), had actually served in World War II and witnessed the real battle firsthand.
The Battle of Britain (1969) used real Spitfires, Hurricanes, and Messerschmitts that had survived the war, along with full-scale replicas.
Battle of Britain featured the largest number of flyable World War II aircraft assembled for a film production, including aircraft acquired from the Spanish Air Force.
War films frequently explore moral ambiguity—soldiers committing atrocities, civilians caught in crossfire, and officers making impossible decisions.
Apocalypse Now and Full Metal Jacket both explore the concept that prolonged exposure to violence can transform even well-intentioned soldiers into something unrecognizable.
For war filmmakers, the challenge of depicting violence responsibly—showing its horror without glorifying it—remains the genre's central artistic tension.
Bitter Victory (1957), directed by Nicholas Ray and starring Richard Burton, explored cowardice and heroism in the North African campaign.
Attack! (1956) directed by Robert Aldrich was one of the first American war films to directly criticize military leadership incompetence.
Hell in the Pacific (1968) starred Lee Marvin and Toshiro Mifune as two enemy soldiers stranded together on a Pacific island—the entire cast of the film.
The Dam Busters (1955), a British film about the famous bouncing bomb raid on German dams in 1943, inspired George Lucas when creating the Death Star trench run in Star Wars.
The bouncing bomb depicted in The Dam Busters was designed by engineer Barnes Wallis and required bombers to fly at exactly 60 feet over water at night.
The Pianist won the Palme d'Or at Cannes in 2002, as well as three Academy Awards.
War films continue to be a vital part of cinema, honoring the sacrifices of those who served while reminding audiences of the devastating human cost of conflict.
